Procedure Note No: 96-18 Date:

 

ber 10, 1996,

 

‘Vemporary Effective Date: Review Date:

Permanent —_—

Type: Product Process Packaging Flavor
Tobacco — Material Specification

SUBJECT: CRES-A Process Specifications

OBJECTIVE: To establish process procedures and specifications for the production of an
ammoniated cut, rolled, expanded flue-cured stem (CRES-A material ).

BACKGROUND: Diammonium phosphate (DAP) has been used to initiate ammonia/sugar
reactions leading to improved taste and slower bum rate for CRES material, CRES treated with
4% DAP ( CRES-A ) is a blend component of a CAMEL LT 85 prototype now being tested for
consumer acceptance. The purpose of this procedure note is to have process procedures and
specifications ready for the production of CRES-A in the event this prototype is selected for
implementation.

RECOMMENDATION: tis recommended that this specification be accepted by the PAC. It
is further recommended that this procedure note be reviewed in 120 days or at the time that the
temporary DAP application system is replaced by a permanent system.

RISK ASSESSMENT: Minimal to no risk associated with this specification. It should be noted
that CRES-A matcrial is produced by an external vendor, CRES Tobacco, Inc., and that
confidential information contained within this document will be accessed by same and will be
outside of the standard RIRT security protocol.

Recommended for Authorization: Dan Pugh (10/10/96)

 

 

 

 

Dan Pugh

inds the use of cadmium in blood as a biomarker of
| review of cadmium, it is stated:

Considerable controversy still s
exposure in the TES pilot study. In the

"Recent exposure would also be indicated by an increase in blood cadmium with peak
concentrations being reached in several minutes and having a half-life of 15 minutes."

Consequently, a biological half-life of 15 mins for cadmium in blood has been assumed for this
biomarker in the pilot study. However, no literature material has been presented to support the
claimed halt-ife of cadmium in blood.

Only limited literature reports the halt-ife of cadmium in blood obtained for workers with
‘occupational exposure to cadmium who were monitored after cessation of exposure, These studies
report mean half-lives of approximately 20.4 months and 31.4 months depending on the initial blood
cadmium level [1], and 2 to 3 months [2]. The halt-life of cadmium has been described to best fit a
two-compartment model with estimated halflife times of 75 to 128 days for the fast component and
7.4 to 16.0 years for the slow component [3].

1. Kelerman, G.R. (1986). Cadmium metabolites in man. Human Toxicol., 5, 91-93.

Peter A. Danelo joined the firm in 1988 and has a general civil litigation practice.

Experience: Mr. Danelo is an experienced trial attorney, emphasizing complex civil
cases and appellate matters. He has been the lead attorney responsible for numerous
class actions, contract disputes, construction cases, product liability claims, fraud
actions, environmental suits, and bankruptcy-related litigation. In his 24-year career, he
has spent approximately 3 1/2 years in trial, including several arbitrations and jury and
non-jury trials in federal and state courts.

Attached is a description of Mr. Danelo’s most significant trials. All but two of the eight
involved the direct and cross-examination by Mr. Danelo of significant technical,
scientific, or engineering experts. Economic or accounting experts also testified in most
of these trials. One of these suits (Asarco adv. Louisiana-Pacific, et al.) was a four-month
jury trial of a products liability case. Mr. Danelo has other products liability experience,
primarily for Cincinnati Milacron, a manufacturer of machine tools. He has represented.
‘tin three actions in the last several years, all of which were successfully disposed of

‘before trial.

Mr. Danelo is a frequent speaker at legal seminars, addressing such topics as federal and
state RICO claims, the use of economic experts in commercial litigation, and seeking or
‘opposing injunctive relief. He has chaired several legal seminars, including one at
‘which most of Seattle's trial and appellate federal judges spoke. He served on the King
County Bar Association's Judicial Screening Committee and the Federal Bar
Association's Continuing Legal Education Committee, and has been a faculty member
of the National Institute for Trial Advocacy.

“The mechanism by which interleukin-l (IL-4) regulates
the expression of particular genes is unknown, We have
‘determined that IL-4 induces a DNA binding factor
(termed STF~TL-4) which has a strong affinity for an
TEN- activation ste (GAS). Interestingly, STF~ IL-4 also
binds to the IL-4 responsive promoter for the Ig heavy
chain germline ¢ transcript. The I-4 dependent
activation of STF ~IL-4is rapid, does not require protein
synthesis and resulls in the sequential appearance of
binding activity first in the cytoplasm and then later in
the nucleus. Activation of STF—IL-4 is sensitive to
tyrosine kinase inhibitors and the active factor is tyrosine
phosphorylated. This pattern of activation is similar to
the activation of interferon-induced transcription factors.
STF-IL-4 appears to be a new member of a growing
family of cytokine-induced transcriptional regulators.
‘Key words: eytokine/interferon/nterlcukin-4/signal trans-
duction

 

 

 

 

 

 

  

Introduction

Inerleukin-4 (IL-4), which i produced by T cells, mast cells
‘and basophils, has potent biological activities’ on many
ifferent cells, including B and TT lymphocytes, mast cells
rx! macrophages (reviewed in Spits, 1992). When cells are
cultured with IL-4, i often stimulates a variety of different
functions within a given cell type. For instance, IL-4 can
stimulate B cells to proliferate and differentiate (reviewed
in Paul and Ohara, 1987), alter the level of cell surface
receptors such as MHC clas Il (Noelle ea, 1984; Roehm
etal, 1984) and CD23 (Defrance eta. 1987; Hudak eal,
1987), and stimulate the B ccls to undergo Ig heavy chain
lass-switching to TgGI and IgE reviewed in Coffman et al.,
1993). Many of these biological activites ely on TL-4'S
ability to stimulate the transcription of particular genes. For
instance, the ability of UL-4 to stimulate Ig heavy chain cass-
switching to IgGI and IgE is dependent on its ability to
stimulate germline +1 and «transcription prior to switching
(reviewed in Coffinan er al., 1993),

‘The gene encoding the IL-4 cellular receptor has been
cloned (reviewed in Lathara etal, 1993) and is a member
of the hematopoietin receptor superfamily (Cosman, 1993).
The gene encodes a 140 000 kDa molecule which contains

1360

‘conserved patter of cysteine residues as well as a WSXWS
box, which are found in other members of this superfamily
‘of cel surface receptors (Miyajima eta, 1992). In alton,
the IL-4 receptor does not contain recognizable kinase
domains, Although the ability of IL-4 to induce the
transcription of certain genes has been well documented, the
mechanism by which the binding of TL-4 10 its receptor
stimulates transcription of these genes remains unknown,

“Murine IL-4 does not seem to activate a cell through the
mobilization of Ca** or by activation ofthe phasphoinositl
pathway (Justement etal, 1986; Mizuguchi etal. , 1986).

Recently, the pathway by which the interferons (IENs)
stimulate transcription of early response genes has been
elucidated (reviewed in Pellegrini and Schindler, 1993). The
binding of TEN (cither a/B oF +) to its receptor stimulates
the activation by tyrosine phosphorylation, of latent
cytoplasmic transcription factors, termed signal transducing
factors (STE) —IFN ad or ~IFN-y, respectively. After this
phosphorylation event, these factors translocate to the nucleus
where, after binding to specific DNA elements [ISRE and
the IFN-» activation site (GAS) respectively in the promoter
‘of responsive genes, they function as transcriptional
‘activators. The components of these STF complexes have
been purified and cloned, and appear to represent a novel
lass of transcriptional activators, One ofthese proteins, p91,
is a component of both STF—IFN-a and STF—IFN-
(reviewed in Pellegrini and Schindler, 1993).

This system of signal transduction is important in the
biological response to IFNs which require the rapid activation
of specific target genes It appears that other cytokines may
employ a related signaling paradigm. For instance, both
CNTF (Bonnie a, 1993) and EGF (Sadowski et al, 1993)
rapidly activate STFs after binding to their respective
receptors. Interestingly, the p9L protein appears to be &
component of both STE—CNTF and STF-EGF (Bonni
cal, 1993; Sivennoinen etal, 1993a,b). Recent evidence
indicates that other cytokines also induce STFS and that
several of these STFS bind toa site in the promoter of the
{RF-I (ISGF2) gene (termed IRF-1 GAS) which has been
shown to bind STF—IFN-» (Pine et al., 1994; P.Rothman
and C.Schindler, unpublished observations)

IL-4 and IFN-y are produced by different subsets of
CD4* helper T cells (reviewed in Mosmann and Coffman,
1989), These two cytokines regulate several steps in B cel
development. One model by which two different cytokines
could regulate the transcription of the same genes would
involve similar pathways of signal transduction. In an effort
to characterize a putative STF that could mediate the effects
‘of IL-4 on B cell development, we determined that IL-4
rapidly activates @ novel STF which has an affinity forthe
GAS element. The activation and activity of this factor
(termed STF IL-4) share many biological features with the
IFN pathway. In addition, STR—IL-4 binds to a sequence
atthe IL-4 inducible promoter of the Ig germline e transcript
that differs significantly from the GAS element.

thoir affinity for adenosine and in their tissue distribution

smokers are regularly exposed to smoke
(15-17), the concentration of noxious
agents in the air exceeds that in inhaled
smoke (1), and a significant amount of nic-
otine is absorbed by exposed nonsmokers
(18, 19). Recent studies suggest poorer pul-
monary function in nonsmokers exposed to
cigarette smoke at work (5), nonsmoking
spouses exposed to smoking mates (6), and
children exposed to smoking mothers (20-
22), and an elevated frequency of respira-
tory tract symptoms in exposed children
(21, 23-25). Epidemiologic studies in

Introduction

‘The biochemical and molecular basis of cancer continues to be
an expanding aea of research. Much ofthe driving force behind
this important mult-disciplinary area is due to concerns about
increasing human health risks caused by exposure to toxic
chemicals ofboth industrial and natural origin. In some instances
the aetiological agents, for example vinyl chloride, as a cause
‘of angiosarcomas in human liver, and tobacco smoke asthe ma-
jor cause of lung cancer, have been identified by clinical and
epidemiological studies involving high-exposure populations.
‘There is, however, an increasing need for reliable prospective
methods for predicting human cancer risks asa result of chemical
exposures. The somatic mutation theory of cancer suggests that
ceancer is caused by genetic damage induced by chemicals or
Viruses, Such mutations resulting from covalent interactions of
chemicals with DNA may be an important inital stage in chemical
‘earcinogenesis, There is now considerable evidence which has
established that many carcinogens or their metabolites covalently
bind to DNA in vivo with the formation of DNA adducts. The
formation of DNA adducts by indirect mechanisms can also occur
ith some carcinogens (Barrows and Skank, 1981). Othe altera-
jons in DNA such as strand breakage (Petzold and Swenberg,
1978), gene rearrangement (Schimke, 1982; Lavi 1982) or gene
amplification (Cairns, 1981; Klein, 1981; Feinberg and Coffey,
1982) may also be important stages in carcinogenesis. However,
the covalent binding of chemicals to DNA appears to be the
significant initiating event which can lad to secondary genomic
alterations unless the carcinogen adduct is repaired: Hence itis
‘widely accepted that any chemical which forms covalent bonds
‘with DNA of somatic and reproductive cells in vivo shouldbe
viewed as a potential mutagen, carcinogen and teratogen. Studies
‘on the binding of chemicals to DNA and the mechanisms of for-
‘mation of DNA adducts are therefore important inthe identifica-
tion of potential carcinogens. In terms of risk assesment, a major
research objective isto determine the qualitative and quantitative
relationships between the formation of DNA adducts and the
resulting lesions in target tissues.

‘Much of our present knowledge onthe interaction between car~
<inogens and cellular macromolecules has been obtained by the
tse of isotopically labelled compounds. Thus, the detection and
{quantitation of the covalent binding of different clases of car-
cinogens to nucleic acids and proteins both in vitro and in vivo
hhas been possible. From such’ studies Lutz (1979, 1982)
developed the concept of the covalent binding index which is
defined as. pmol of chemical bound per mole of DNA
rvcleotide/mamol of chemical per kg body wt of animal. However,
this concept does not take qualitative features into account and
itis now generally accepted that covalent binding shoud be deter-
‘mined at the level of DNA adducts. Thus in most studies radio-

‘IRL Pres Limited, Oxford. England

 

 

labelled carcinogens have been administered to rodents and the
presence of reaction products with DNA has been determined
‘fer isolation of DNA from various orga, usually liver. follow
‘ed by hydrolysis of the DNA. The identity of DNA adducts in
the hydrolysate is usually determined by co-chromatography of
the hydrolysate bn h.p..c. with appropriate syrbetic reference
‘compounds. inthis way DNA adducts ofa variety of carcinogens
including alkylating agents, polycytic aromatic hydrocarbons,
aromatic amines and mycototins have been identified. Modified
DNA is usually enzymically digested to constituent deoxyribo-
rucleosdes which are then analysed for adducts. Some limit.
tions can arise due to incomplete digestion or instability of
adducts.

“The application ofthese techniques is limited to those com-
pounds which may be synthesized readily in radiolabelled form
and the specific radioactivity achievable determines the limit of
detection of adducts in vivo. Studies in humans using these
methods are not possible. As a result, alternative methods for
“dying interactions of carcinogens with DNA which donot rely
‘on radiolabelled carcinogens have been developed. At the
forefront of such methods for detection of DNA adducts formed
from non radiolabelled compounds are immunochemical assays,
and post-radiolabellng, The incorporation of high- specific radio-
activity inthe form of "P into DNA adducts has tus far found
widest application of the later technique.

p.post-labelling method for the analysis of carcinogen—DNA
adducts (K Randerath ea, 1981, 1984, 1985a; Gupta eal,
1982). The procedure is exci a development of Randerath’s
‘methodology for base composition (K.Randerath ef al., 1980)
and sequence analysis (K-Randerath eral, 1980; Maxam and
Gilbert, 1981; Sanger, 1981) of nucleic acids. The basic pro-
cedure involves the incorporation of “P into non-radioactive
nucleic acid constituents by an enzyme-catalysed derivatization
followed by chromatographic separation of radioactive products.
“The most widely used chromatographic method hasbeen multi
dimensional thin layer chromatography (..) though the use
of hps.c. has also been reported (Haseltine et al, 1983).
‘Aubough several modifications have now been incorporated in
to the method, and these are described below (and summarized
in Figure 1), the exental procedure i 2s follows. DNA is isolated
from tissues of animals exposed to suspect chemical carcinogens
and then digested enzymically to deaxyribonvcleoside 3”-mono-
phosphates with micrococeal miclease and spien exonuclease.

"The protective role of cigarette smoking during exposure to pollutant:

 

“Hypotheses have energed regarding the protective role of ciga~
rette snoking during exposures to pollutants, viz, that clearance of
podutants is facilitated following suoking; mucus produced by cigarette
snoke exposures provides a shield to underlying sensitive cells; end/or
cigarette saoking produces a thickening of bronchial epithelium which
also shields underlying sensitive cells. Data exanining these hypotheses
ate derived from previous studies of dogs exposed to radon, uranium
ofe dust and cigarette suoke. In these studies it was noted that sig-
nificantly fever lung cancers occurred in the snoking group otherwise
simtlarly exposed to radon and uranium ore dust. Similar carcinogenic
data exist in certain miner populations. Histopathologic and morpho-
metric data regarding mucus cell populations and epithelial thickness
im the trachea and bronchi of these animals will be presented,"

Keith Burridge,* Christopher E. Turner,! and Lewis H. Romer’

Abstract. Celts in culture reveal high levels of protein
tyrosine phosphorylation in their focal adhesions, the
regions where cells adhere to the underlying substra-
tum, We have examined the tyrosine phosphorylation
of proteins in response to plating cells on extracellular
matrix substrata. Rat embryo fibroblasts, mouse Balb/c
313, and NIH 373 cells plated on fibronectin-coated
surfaces revealed elevated phosphotyrosine levels in a
cluster of proteins between 115 and 130 kD. This in-
crease in tyrosine phosphorylation was also seen when
rat embryo fibroblasts were plated on laminin or vitro-
nectin, but not on polylysine or on uncoated plastic
Integrin mediation of this effect was suggested by
finding the same patter of elevated tyrosine phos-
phorylation in cells plated on the ceil-binding frag-
‘ment of fibronectin and in cells plated on a synthetic
polymer containing multiple RGD sequences. We have

 

identified one of the proteins of the 115-130-KD clus-
ter as ppi25™*, a tyrosine kinase recently localized in
focal adhesions (Schaller, M. D., C. A. Boreman,
B.S. Cobb, R. R. Vines, A. B. Reynolds, and J. T.
Parsons. 1992. Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA. 89:5192)..
{A second protein that becomes tyrosine phos-
pphorylated in response to extracellular matrix adhe-
sion is identified as paxillin, a 70-kD protein previ-
ously localized (0 focal adhesions. Treatment of cells
with the tyrosine kinase inhibitor herbimycin A dimin-
ished the adhesion-induced tyrosine phosphorylation of
these proteins and inhibited the formation of focal
adhesions and stress fibers. These results suggest a
role for integrin-mediated tyrosine phosphorylation ia
the organization of the cytoskeleton as cells adhere to
the extracellular matrix.

 

‘of cell behavior, including the migratory properties of,

cells, their morphology, growth characteristics, and
differentiation (Hay, 1981; Hynes, 1990). The means by
‘which the ECM exerts these effects are largely unknown, but
there is evidence that many of these actions are mediated
through integrins, the family of ECM receptors found on
‘most cells. Integrins are a@ heterodimers; each subunit has
a large extracellular domain, spans the membrane once, and
hasa short cytoplasmic sequence (Buck and Horwitz, 1987;
Hynes, 1987; Ruoslahti and Pierschbacher, 1987). Multiple
and subunits have been identified (Albelda and Buck,
1990; Hemler, 1990; Hynes, 1992). Some subunits are shared
bby many integrins (e-g., 8:), whereas others appear to be
‘unique, During the last few’ years much has been learned
‘about the interaction of integrins with particular ECM com-
‘ponents such as fibronectin or vitronectin, In many cells in
‘culture, integrins are clustered at sites of adhesion to the un-
derlying ECM in regions known as focal adhesions (focal
contacts or adhesion plaques) (for review, see Burridge et

attachment to matrix components adsorbed onto the plastic
or glass culture surface, whereas the cytoplasmic face pro-
vides a site of attachment for bundles of actin filaments
(stress fibers). Evidence from in vitro binding experiments
indicates that intogrins can interact with atleast two cyto-
skeletal proteins, talin (Horwitz et al., 1986) and a-actinin
(rey et al., 1990). Along with several other cytoskeletal
proteins, talin and q-actinin are concentrated at the cyto-
plasmic face of focal adhesions (Burridge et al., 1988).
Focal adhesions are thought to be important not only as
structural links between the ECM and the eytoskeleton, but
also as sites of signal transduction from the ECM. Several
potential regulatory enzymes have been identified in focal
adhesions (Burridge et al., 1988). These include protein ki
nase C Gaken et al., 1989) and several tyrosine kinases
(Rohrschneider, 1980; Robrschneider and Gentry, 1984;
Rohrschneider and Najita, 1984). Considerable attention
hhas been directed toward the tyrosine kinase p60",
which is localized to the residual focal adhesions of cells
transformed by Rous sarcoma virus (Rohrschneider, 1980).
p60* has not yet been detected in the focal adhesions of|
normal cells, but elevated phosphotyrosine has been noted

NCREASING evidence demonstrates that extracel
lular matrix can influence cell adhesion, shape, po-
'Y, growth, migration, metabolism, and differentia-

tion in vitro (1, 2). In the 10-day-old developing tests,

the seminiferous cords consist of early germ cells and

Sertoli cells (3), which are tall columnar cells with a

highly polarized cytoplasm (Fig. 1). Sertoli cells are in

contact at their basal surface with a basement membrane
matrix composed of laminin, type IV collagen, heparan
sulfete proteoglycan, and possidly entactin (4). When

Sertoli cells from 10-day-old rats are cultured on plastic,
they exhibit a squamous morphology and generally lack

the polarity observed in vivo. On a laminin substrate, the

Sertoli cells become somewhat more differentiated and

assume a cuboidal to low columnar shape (Fig. 1). How
ever, when cultured on a Matrigel substrate, the Sertoli
cells adopt a phenotype very similar to that of the in vivo
10-day-old Sertoli cells. They appear tall and columnar
and are highly polarized with basally located nuclei and.
abundant supranuclear cytoplasm (3) (Fig. 1). Sertoli
cells cultured on Matrigel also secrete more total protein
and more androgen-binding protein and transferrin than
cells grown on plastic (5). Little is known about the
intracellular signals involved in the Sertoli cell response
to basement membrane. In fact, the signal mechanisms
for cellular responses to extracellular matrix components
in most other cell systems have not been adequately
studied,

Sertoli cell function is under the control of the gonad-
otropin FSH (6, 7). FSH binding to plasma membrane
receptors stimulates adenylyl cyclase and, subsequently,
cAMP production, the postulated second messenger of
FSH action in Sertoli cells (6, 7). Activation of adenylyl
cyclase by the FSH-receptor complex is mediated by the

todide and Related Compounds at Vascula

Abstract—5-Methylfurfuryltrimethylamnionium iodide (5-MFT) is a
furan analog of muscarine and was studied for its cholinergic
activity at vascular endothelial receptors of the rat aorta, Other
related compounds have different substitutions at position 5 of
the furan ring and include the following compounds: $-hydroxy-
methyl-_(5-HMFT), S-chloromethyl- (5-CMFT). 5-bromomethyl.
(S-BMFT), 5-iodomethyl- (5-IMFT), and $-methoxy- (5-MOFT)
furfuryltrimethylammonium salts. The furan analogs relaxed helical
strips of rat aorta which contracted with norepinephrine (10-* M).
These relaxations were endothelial cell-dependent. The EDs's for
muscarinic activities increased in the following order: S-MFT = ACh
<di-muscarine < S-HMFT = 5-CMFT < 5-MOFT < 5-BMFT.
<5-IMFT. Among the furan analogs, 5-MFT was found to be a full
agonist at the endothelial cells; other furan analogs were only partial
agonists. The affinities and relative intrinsic efficacies of the must
Potent analogs decreased in the following order: ACh=5-MFT >
dtemuscarine > 5-H MFT > 5-CMFT.

Atropine and scopolamine antagonized relaxations by furan
analogs. Kp values for atropine and scopolamine against ACh,
S-MFT or 5-HMFT as agonist were not different. indicating that
these agonists and antagonists were acting at the same muscarinic
Teceptors. The Ky of atropine and of scopolamine increased when
S-CMFT was used as an agonist, indicating that $-CMFT may
cause relaxation by acting at other sites besides endotheliat
muscarinic receptors. The endothelial muscarinic receptor might be
classified tentatively as of Mz or M, type. These studies did not
exclude the possible heterogeneity of the endothelial muscarinic

receptors.

ACTIVATION OF PROTEIN KINASE C BY 12(S)-HETE: ROLE IN TUMOR CELL METASTASIS

B.LIU, C. RENAUD, J. KOWYNIA, K. K. NELSON, E. ROUDACHEVSKI, D. SNYDER, J. TIMAR and K. V.
HONN,

Departments of Radiation Oncology and Chemistry, Wayne State University, and Gershenson Radiation
Oncology Center, Harper Hospital, Detroit, Mi, 48202, USA

INTRODUCTION

Protein kinase C (PKC), the Ca2+ and phospholipid dependent kinase, belongs to the family of
protein serine/threorine kinases. It is an important enzyme in mediating cell growth, cell diferentiation
‘and tumor promotion (1, 2). Physiologically, PKC is activated by diacylglycerol (DAG) generated during
Phosphatidyinosito! (Pl) turover, Metabolism of Pl is usually initiated by the action of phospholipase C in
response to extracellular factors including hormones, growth factors and neurotransmitter. In addition to
OAG, inositol trisphosphate (IP) is generated during Pl turnover. While DAG activates PKC, IP
‘moblizes intracellular Ca2*(3), Tumor promoting phorbol esters, such as phorbol 12-myristate-12-acotate
(PMA), directly activates PKC by a mechanism similar to that of DAG (3)

12(S)-hydroxyelcosatetraenoic acid (12(S)-HETE], a lipoxygenase metabolite of arachidonic acid,

* mimics the effect of PMA and increases surtace expression ofthe allbB3 integrin and enhances tumor
cell adhesion to endothelium and subendothelial matrix (4). This implies a protein kinase G mediated
‘mechanism of action for 12(S)-HETE. In fact, a numberof fatty acids including arachidonic acid and some
of its metabofites [including 12(S)-HETE] are able to directly activate protein kinase C in vito (6-8).
Recently we reported that 12(S)-HETE increased adhesion to endothelium of rat Walker 256
‘carcinosarcoma (W256) ces by activation of PKC (10).

‘Tumor cell metastasis is a multi-step process involving interactions among tumor cells and host
calls (11, 12). We reported that subpopulations of cels, differing in their lung cotonization potentials,
Could be isolated from B16a amelanotic melanoma (B16a) tumors by centrifugal elutiation (13). A
Positive correlation has been observed among lung colonization potential (13), ability to induce platelet
Aagregation (13), plasma membrane expression of allbBS integrin (14) and levels of cysteine proteinases
cathepsin B and L (15, 16) in these B16a subpopulations. in this study, we compared the levels of PKC
and 12(S)-HETE in these subpopulations. We also studied the effect of 12(S)-HETE on their adhesion
{0 the matrix protein fibronectin. Finally, the involvement of PKC in adhesion to fibronectin was
investigated by using protein kinase inhibitors

Neil E. Caporaso,* Margaret A. Tucker, Robert N. Hoover, Richard B
Hayes, Linda W. Pickle, Haleem J. Issag, Gary M. Muschik, Lauren
Green-Gallo, Daina Buivys, Sena Aisner, James H. Resau, Benjamin F.
Trump, David Tollerud, Ainsley Weston, Curtis C. Harris.

 

Ina case-control study, we tested the hypothesis that the
genetically determined ability to metabolize debrisoquine is
related to risk of hung cancer. Overall; individuals who were
‘extensive metabolizers of debrisoquine were at significantly
‘greater risk of kung cancer than those who were poor or
intermediate metabolizers (odds ratio = 6.1; 95% confidence
interval = 2.2-17.1). In this study, case patients had lung
cancer, and coatrolisubjects had either chronic obstructive
pulmonary disease or cancers other than tung cancer. Results
were adjusted forage, race, asbestos exposure, and smoking.
Both black and white individuals who were extensive metab-
olizers of debrisoquine were at significantly increased risk
after similar adjustment (for blacks, odds ratio = 4.5, 95%
‘confidence interval = 1.1-18.1; for whites, odds ratio = 10.2,
‘95% confidence interval = 2.0-S1.4) Significantly increased
risk of lung cancer was also present for individuals who were
‘extensive metabolizers when subjects with chronic obstruc-
tive pulmonary disease or other cancers were considered
‘separately. These data confirm thatthe ability to metabolize
debrisoquine is a major determinant of susceptibility to lung
cancer. Evaluation of the marker in other case-control
settings, further exploration of racial differences, and the
[prospective evaluation of this marker in subgroups at high
risk of lung cancer are areas worthy of forther study. [J Natl
(Cancer Inst $2:1264-1272, 1990)

 

 

While exposure to tobacco smoke is widely accepted as the
‘major etiologic factor in lung cancer, differences in individual
susceptibility have been inferred from the observation thal only a
minority of cigarette smokers have diagnoses of lung cancer
(1.2). aviation inthe ability to metabolize xenobiotics has been
‘considered as possible explanation for this phenomenon (3), 3nd
thishypothesiss consistent with family and win stadies (4-6), 5
‘well as cytogenetic and molecular investigations (7-12) that have
indicated a roe for genetic predisposition in lng cancer etiology

‘The metabolism of the antihypertensive drug debrisoguine is
‘under autosomal genetic control (13~15) and inheritance ofthe
tran conferring ability o “extensively” metabolize the drug has
been suggested as a host susceptibility factor for lung-cancer
(16.17). In an eatier sudy comparing patients with ling cancer
and patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, white
British cigareite smokers with ihe extensive debrisoquine-metad-

 

olizer phenotype were at higher risk of developing bronchial
carcinoma than were intermediate and poor metabolizers of the
rug. One concem in’ interpreting these data was the use of
subjects with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease as 4 sole
‘comparison group. Other studies considering this association
have produced conflicting results (/8,19) or have atracted crt-
cismof the epidemiologic methods (20:2)

To examine the hypothesis of an association of risk of lung
cancer with the ability wo metabolize debrisoquine, we conducted
«formal epidemiologic case-control study. We used two separate
‘comparison groups and adjusied results for recognized isk
factors for lung cancer. This work focuses on the assessment of|
genetically determined differences in the ability to metabolize
<ebrisoquine as an approach to elucidating a genetic component
of ung cancer susceptibility.

Introduction

The major iron-storage protein, ferritin, plays a
Key role in iron metabolism by maintaining a
reserve supply of this essential metal inside cells
(1,2). Iron is stored in ferritin as a microcrystalline
hydrated ferric oxide [3,4]; the mineralized iron is
surrounded by an outer protein shell representing
a polymerized complex of 24 polypeptide subunits
(external diameter = 130 A) 1,2}. This unique
molecular architecture keeps the iron in a soluble
and non-toxic state, When confronted with
parenteral iron overload (siderosis), the existing
population of molecules sequesters. increased
amounts of iron, and cells are induced to rapidly

All unfractionated populations of pure ferritin
show a prominent protein heterogeneity, particu-
larly with regard to molecular charge [1,8-10]
Numerous isomeric forms (isoferritins) are nor-
rally present in different tissues (8-11), and even
within some single types of cells [12,13] The many
charge isomers of ferritin all seem to be het-
ceropolymers made from characteristic proportions
of two classes of polypeptide subunits: H-type (M,
21000), which is prominent in heart ferritin, and
Ltype (M, 19000), which is enriched in ferritin

p53 in benzo[a]pyrene-treated
transgenic p53-deficient mice

DNA damage may mediate birth defects caused by many drugs and environmental
chemicals, therefore p63, a tumour suppressor gene that facilitates DNA repair, may be
critically embryoprotective. We have studied the effects of the environmental teratogen,
benzolalpyrene, on pregnant heterozygous p83-deficient mice, Such mice exhibited
between 2- to 4-fold higher embryotoxicity and teratogenicity than normal p53-controls.
Fetal resorptions reflecting in utero death were genotyped using the polymerase chain
reaction and found to be increased 2.6-fold and 3.6-fold respectively with heterozygous
and homozygous p63-deficient embryos. These results provide the first direct evidence
that p53 may be an important teratological suppressor gene which protects the embryo
from DNA-damaging chemicals and developmental oxidative stress.

‘To gain a better understanding of the factors that can
mediate chromatin assembly, we have purified and
cloned a core histone-binding protein from Drosophila
melanogaster embryos. This protein resembles Xenopus
laevis nucleoplasmin, and it has therefore been termed
ANLP, for Drosophila nucleoplasmin-like protein. ANLP.
is.a nuclear protein that is present throughout develop-
‘ment. Both purified native and recombinant dNLP bind
to core histones and can function in the assembly of
approximately regularly spaced nucleosomal arrays in a
reaction that additionally requires DNA, purified core
histones, ATP, and a partially purified fraction (contain-
ing at least one other assembly activity). We also ana-
lyzed the properties of an N-terminally truncated ver
sion of NEP, termed dNLP-S, and found that the
Geletion of the N-terminal 31 residues of dNLP results
in a loss of the specificity of the Interaction of
ANLP with core histones. We then compared the abili-
ties of dNLP and Drosophila nucleosome assembly pro-
tein-l (HNAP-1) to promote the decondensation of Xeno-
‘pus sperm chromatin, « process that can be mediated by
‘nucleoplasmin. We observed that dNAP-1, but not dNLP,
‘was able to promote the decondensation of sperm chro-
matin. These and other data collectively suggest that
NLP may participate in parallel with other histone
binding proteins such as dNAP-1 in the assembly of
chromati

   

 

 

 

 

 

Chromatin assembly is a fundamental process that is in-
-velved in broad range of biological phenomena such as gene
regulation, recombination, DNA repair, and progression
through the call cycle, and itis therefore important to investi
ita the factors that participate in the formation of chromatin
(for reviews, se Refs. 1-7). The analysis of chromatin assembly
thas revealed that the deposition of the core histones f{3 and H4
precedes the incorporation of H2A and 12B into chromatin,
find that both the pre-existing and newly synthesized histones
fre randomly distributed among the daughter DNA strands.

Moreover, the newly synthesiaed histones, which are more
highly acetylated in their lysine-rich N-terminal tails relative
tobulk histones, become deacetylated after assembly into chro-
matin, Chromatin assembly commences immediately after
DNA replication, and DNA replication and chromatin assembly
appear to be eoupled, although possibly by an indirect mecha-
nism, as chromatin assembly appears to occur preferentially
bat not vbligatrily, with newly replicated DNA (see, for in-
stance, Ref. 8).

Biochemical studies of chromatin assembly have led to the
identification of several core histone-binding fuctors, which
inelude nucleoplasmain (9-19), NI/N2 (14-17), nucleosome as
sembly protein-1 (NAP-1)' (18-21), and chromatin assembly
factor-I (CAF-1) (22-24), The current data suggest that nucleo-
plasmin and NAP-1 interact proferentilly with H2A and H2B
relative to H3 and 114 (17, 19, 21) and that NUN2 and CAP-1
bind to H3 and H4 (14, 15, 17, 23, 24), These factors may
therefore act, atleast in part, as histone chaperones that de-
liver the core histones to the nesely replicated DNA. Moreover,
because of their related biochemical properties, itis possible
that there may be some redundancy in the function of these
histone-binding factors in chromatin assembly.

‘The mechanism of chromatin assembly is likely, however, 0
‘be more complex than the random deposition of histones that is
mediated by the core histone-binding factors alone. For in-
stance, it is known from studies of chromatin assembly acti
ties in rude extracts derived from Xenopus laeuis oocytes (23),
HeLa cells (26), or Drosophila melanogaster embryos (21, 28)
that the assembly of approximately regularly spaced nucleoso-
mal arrays is an ATP-dopendent process. Biochemical fraction-
ation of a chromatin assembly extract from Drosophila ex:
bryos led to the identification of two fractions, termed ACAF-L
fand €CAF-4, which, when combined, were able to reconstitute
the ATP-faciitated assembly of nucleosomal arrays, as was
observed with the crude extract (29). One component in the
ACAF-4 fraction was purified and cloned, and found to be the
Drosophila homologue of NAP-1 (NAP-1) (21). Purified recom-
binant ANAP-1 was observed to function ina cooperative man-
nner with the active componentis) in the €CAR-1 fraction to
mediate the ATP-faciitated assembly of nucleosomal arrays
‘The ATP-utilizing chromatin assembly factors) in the ACAP-1
fraction has not yet been identified, although itis known that
‘the Drosophila homologue of CAF-1 (termed CAP-1 protein,
which should not be confused with tho @CAF-1 fraction) is

anti-bovine thymocyte serum (GABTS), but only
with 25% of GABTS-positive thymocytes. Immuno-
Peroxidase staining of frozen tissue sections

vine thymocytes,

by Andrew T. Lendtoay and Tibor S. Laszio

INTRODUCTION

A large body of experimental evidence supports the
dependence of cigarette smoke composition on tem-
perature. Phenol formation, during the Pyrolysis of
‘wood lignin, increased fourfold between 4c0®C and
600° C but decreased more than tenfold between 600°C
and 900°C (a), In a study of the pyrolysis of tobacco
Teaf constituents (2), it was found that yields of benzene
and biphenyl increase “dramatically” when the tem-
perature increases from 420° C to 620°C. As the tem-
perature approaches 820°C, the yield of fused-ring
aromatics (indene, acenaphthene, ete) also increases.
A patented process (5) daims to reduce the polycyclic
Ihydrocarbons in smoke by increasing the combustion
temperature. The temperature increase can be achieved,
it is claimed, by the addition of alkaline earth per-
formates to the tobacco. Another patent (4) proposes
the reduction of temperature from 880° C to 720°C by
the addition of borates, phosphates and silicates in
order to reduce the aromatic hydrocarbon content of the
smoke,

Significant to this problem is work that was performed
on the pyrolysis of organic compounds. Lam (5) ob-
tained 5,4-benzoyrene and other aromatic compounds
from aliphatic hydrocarbons heated at 700°—800° C; at
600°C, no such compounds were formed. The same
author reported quantitative correlations between
pyrolysis temperature and the generation of certain
compounds (6). Heating 500 mg of tobacco paraffins
at 850°C produced 2 mg 5,4-benzpyrene, and at a
higher temperature (970° C) only 637 mg.

To these date, related to cigarette smoke, can be added
the large’ volume of available information pertaining
to the effect of temperature on the products of thermal
cracking of petroleum fractions

‘THE MEASUREMENT METHOD

For this investigation of cigarette peak coal tempera-
tures, a recently developed scanning infrared technique
was used (7). This technique is based on the theore-
tically derived assumption that the cigarette coal has
an emittance close to unity. Tests were carried out to
support this assumption.

Hypoxic Stimulation of Vascular Endothelial
Growth Factor Expression In vitro and In vivo

ALEXANDER MINCHENKO, THOMAS BAUER, SUSANA SALCEDA, AND JAIME CaRO

Department of Medicine, Cardeza Foundation for Hematologic Research, Jefferson Medical
College of Thomas Jefferson University, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania

BACKGROUND: Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) is a specific endothelial cell mitogen
with potent angiogenic properties. In tumors, VEGF has been localized to the most necrotic and
ischemic areas of the tissues, suggesting that local hypoxia is a potent inducer of VEGF production,
Initial experiments in vitro confirmed the stimulatory effect of hypoxia on VEGF expression. The
extent of this response and the mechanisms involved in oxygen sensing are poorly cheracterized.

EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN: Confluent monolayers of malignant cell lines or primary cultures of
fibroblast or endothelial cells were exposed to hypoxia or incubated with either cobalt chloride,
stimulator of erythropoietin gene expression, or sodium azide, an inhibitor of oxydative phos
phorylation. VEGF expression was analyzed by Northern blot or RNase protection assays. The
expression VEGF in vivo was studied in animals subjected to hypobaric hypoxia or functional
anemia.

RESULTS: Hypoxia greatly stimulated VEGF expression in tumor cell lines and primary fibroblast
cultures, Endothelial cells, that expressed very low constitutive levels of VEGE, were resistant to
hypoxic stimulation. RNase protection analysis showed that hypoxia primarily stimulated the
induction of smaller and medium VEGF isoforms, é.e., the same ones expressed under normal
conditions. The stimulatory effect of hypoxia on VEGF could be reproduced in vitro by cobalt
chloride but not with sodium azide. Zn vivo, both hypoxia and anemia were found to be potent
inducers of VEGF expression in several organs including heart, brain, liver, kidney, and muscle.
As in vitro, cobalt was also found to be a potent stimulator of VEGF in vivo,

CONCLUSIONS: Hypoxia is a potent inducer of VEGF expression in malignant as well as normal
cultured cells. It is also a stimulator of VEGF expression in vivo. The VEGF gene appears to
respond to hypoxia like the erythropoietin gene, and the mechanism of oxygen sensing probably is

   

 

 

 

 

mediated by a heme-containing protein.

Additional key words: Hypoxia, Angiogenesis, Erythropoietin.

Angiogenesis, the development of new microvascular
networks, is an important phenomenon associated with
several physiologic conditions such as wound healing,
compensatory tissue hyperplasias, and endometrial pro-
liferation in response hormones of the menstrual cycle
(1), Angiogenesis also plays a significant role in a variety
of pathologic disorders including neoplasms, chronic in-
flammation, retinopathies, and in pannus formation es-
sociated with rheumatoid arthritis (1). The development
‘of new vasculature appears to be an absolute requirement
for tumor growth and expansion (2)

Among the several substances reported to induce an-
siogenesis, vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) is
one of the most intimately involved in neovascularization
(3). Also known as vascular permeability factor, VEGF
is @ homodimeric glycoprotein that specifically acts on
endothelial cells (3). Due to alternative splicing of its
primary mRNA transcript, four isoforms of VEGF are
produced (4). These different forms differ in their bio-
availability due to their different capacity of interaction
with the extracellular matrix (5),

VEGF messenger is found temporally and spatially

 

 

related to new blood vessel formation in developing
tissues, in the ovary and in the healing wound (6-8).
In tumars, VEGF was found to be localized to the
most ischemic areas of the tissues, suggesting that
local hypoxia may be a potent inducer of its pro-
duction (8). Indeed, exposure of glioma cells to hy-
poxia in vitro resulted in dramatic increase in VEGF
expression (9). ‘The mechanism of oxygen sensing
and the response of the VEGF gene to hypoxia are
still unclear, but could involve a heme-containing
protein sensor, such as the one described in the act-
ivation of the erythropoietin gene (10). This heme-
protein seems to be present in most cells and itis likely
to be part of a generalized mechanism of oxygen
sensing (11-13). We have attempted here to further
characterize the effects of hypoxia on the VEGF gene
in normal and malignant cell lines and also to ex-
plore its effects in vivo. In addition, experimental
evidence is presented to suggest that hypoxia sensing by
the VEGF gene utilizes a heme-containing protein, prob.
ably similar to the one utilized in the hypoxia response
of the erythropoietin gene

INTRODUCTION

‘The interpretation of thoracic radiographs
for pulmonary abnormalities does not ust-
ally provide a definite diagnosis. Al-
though certain radiographic signs are as-
sociated commonly with specific diseases,
very few ate pathognomonic. ‘Therefore,
the most reliable approach to ‘interpret-
ing thoracic radiographs utili
recognition.” Pulmonary lesions and dis-
‘eases with a common morphological basis,
may be manifested as a repeatedly identi-
fiable radiographic pattern (1). Though
non-specific, the recognition of a radio-
graphic pattern provides a differential
diagnosis which can be offered to account
for physical signs of disease or to sub-
stantiate a clinical impression.

‘The purpose of this paper is to present
the features of a radiographic pattern of
pulmonary disease characterized by an in-
crease in linear markings. In order to ap-
preciate the close correlation between the
tissue changes and their radiographic
manifestation, the morphologic features of
these diseases will be illustrated simul-
taneously.

 

LITERATURE REVIEW

In an earlier publication, an increase in
linear lung markings was described as
constituting a feature of the radiographic
appearance of the lungs of aged dogs.
Pathologically, the increase in linear mark-
ings was correlated with focal alveolar
fibrosis and scarring, and with calcification
of bronchial cartilage. ‘These changes were

not associated regularly with physical
signs of pulmonary disease (12).

‘An approach to the classification of
radiographic patterns in diffuse pulmonary
disease has appeared recently. In this
report the authors offer a system of clas-
sification consisting of four patterns; al-
veolar,, interstitial, bronchovascular, and
mixed (16). Linear markings, as defined in
the following description, would fall into
the interstitial and bronchovascular pat-
terns.

‘The human medical literature contains
numerous references to a radiographic pat-
tern consisting of an increase in pulmonary
linear markings. Several have been as-
sociated with specific disease entities such
as chronic bronchitis (11), diffuse inter-
stitial fibrosis (7), segmental atelectasis
(©), and pulmonary hemosiderosis (6).
A recently published textbook of radiology
contains a comprehensive bibliography (8).
Older texts also devote considerable at-
tention to this pattern of disease (3)
‘The mechanisms by which linear radi-
opacities are produced.arg found.in.a paper
which discusses the radiologic differential
diagnosis of pulmonary disease in man (1).

aminations were performed at a field sta-
tion with portable radiographic equipment

S. Chacko and P. A. Longhurst

Summary. Unlike striated muscle cells, smooth muscle
cells do not have an organized sarcomeric structure. How-
ever, all smooth muscle cells contain the contractile pro-
teins, myosin, atin, and tropomyosin. Polymorphism of
the myosin heavy chain exists in smooth muscle eels
‘Two myosin heavy chain (MHC) isoforms, SMI (204
kDa) and SM2 (200 kDa), are present in smooth muscle
cells; however, their ratios vary in smooth muscles from
different sources. The hypertrophy of the urinary bladder
induced by partial outlet obstruction in rabbits s associ
ated with an alteration of the SMI-to-SM2 ratio from 1:3
to 1:1. Both heavy chains resct with polyclonal antibody
against smooth muscle myosin; however, antibody. pre
pared against a peptide from the C-terminal region of the
SM2 heavy chain cross-reacts only with the SM2 heavy
chain, Removal ofthe obstruction reverses the bladder to
normal mass with a concomitant change in the SMI-to-
SM? ratio back to 1:3. The expression of the SMI mRNA
is increased in response to obstruction-induced hypertro-
phy, and it also returns to normal upon removal of the ob-
struction. Urinary bladder smooth muscle contains pre-
dominantly y-actin. Obstruction-induced hypertrophy of
the bladder smooth muscle is associated with an increase
inthe yactin a both protein and mRNA levels. The Benoa
muscle actin is decreased and the a-smooth muscle Metin
is unchanged in response to cbstruction-induced bladder
hypertrophy.

Contraction of all smooth muscles involves similar
mechanisms. This review describes our current under-
standing of the mechanisms regulating contraction of the
smooth muscle of the urinary bladder.

Bradford 8. Schwartz

Abstract

“The present study explores the interactions between lymphocytes
and monocytes that ure required for expression of procoagulant
activity (PCA) by monocytes in response to purified protein de-

ivative ofthe tubercle bacillus (PPD) or tularemia antigen. The
PCA response was antigen specific: peripheral blood monon
clear cells (PBM) from donors sensitive to PPD or tularemia
showed an increase in PCA only in response to the sensitizing
antigen. The PCA was tissue factorike in that Factors VIL and
X were required for expression of the activity, whereas Factor
VIII was not. Maximum PCA developed only after 36 t0 72 b.
Fractionation of PBM into lymphocytes and monocytes after
antigenic stimulation demonstrated that >90% of the PCA was
ssociated with monocytes. Isolated monocytes or lymphocytes
incubated with sensitizing antigen had the same PCA as control
cells. Purified lymphocytes that had been pulsed with antigen
were unable to elicit a PCA response from monocytes t0 which
they were added. Howerer, adherent monocytes incubated with
antigen, then washed free of unbound protein, were able to trigger
Iymphocytes to become stimulatory for PCA toward responding
‘monocytes. The development of antigen-specific PCA in PBM
‘could be blocked by including a monoctonal antibody to HLA~
DR antigen in the incubation. The antibody had no effect onthe
lorting assay, on preformed PCA, cell viability, or on stimulatory
antigen itself. These results indicate that elaboration of PCA by
‘mononuclear cells may bean iatrinsic part of the classical immune
response to antigen, and may explain the presence of fibrin in
immune lesions, as well as the occurrence of thrombotic com
plications in many immune disorders.

 

 

 

 

  

   

Introduction

Cellemediated immunity i typified by the delayed type byper-
sensitivity (DTH)! reaction. A hallmark of the lesion isthe de-
‘position of fibrin which aocounts forthe characteristic induration
distinguishing positive from negative reactions (1, 2). It seems
unlikely that nonspecific tissue damage is the initiator ofthis
‘coagulation, In the model of experimental allergic encephalo-
rmyelitis, one of the earliest identifiable abnormalities is fibrin

<eposition (3). This argues for coagulation occurring relatively
carly in the course of immune lymphocyte activation. Such
|ymphocyte activation in response to protein antigen requires
antigen presentation by monocyte/macrophages (4) or dendritic
‘els (5), although other ces (6, 7) can also perform this function,
Antigen presenting cells share the feature of expressing lalike
antigens on ther surface (6-8) The la protein provides the con-
text for T cells to recognize foreign antigens, and it is here that
the specificity of the immune respoase may be controlled (8)

A likely mediator of coagulation in immune-mediated re-
actions isthe procoagulant activity (PCA) that can be induced
to appear on the surface of cells of the monocyte/macrophage
lineage (9). Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBM) can be
induced to produce large amounts of PCA in response to mi-
togenic letins (10), bacteria! lipopalysaccha-ide (LPS) (11), n-
tigen antibody complexes (12), proteolytic products of comple-
ment activation (13), sensitizing protein antigens (14), and al-
logeneic stimuli (15). The PCA is expressed and contained by
‘monocytes, not lymphocytes (16-18), and is cell bound, not
released into the medium (18, 19). To date, cellular interactions
between lymphocytes and monocytes that culminate in mono-
cyte PCA production have been described in systems with poly
clonal stimuli that do not require specific recognition (16-18).
[Neither LPS or antigen/antibody complexes have been shown
to have a role in DTH: and the time course of monocyte PCA
production in response to these stimuli (6 h) isnot at alin tine
‘with the time course of DTH reactions (24~48 h). The present
studies, therefore, addressed the cellular events required for
‘expression of monocyte PCA in response toa sensitizing protein
antigen, The celular pathways were found to differ markedly
from those previously described in LPS or immune complex
induced PCA. In addition, the time course ofthe process cor-
related nicely with the appearance of fibrin in in vivo cell-me-
diated immune tissve lesions.

centration of vasoprestin (VP) is reduced (Lang etal, 1981;
Mortis and Keller, 1982). However, the basal levels of adren-
cergic amines such as epinephrine, angiotensin II (Nagaoka and
Lovenbere, 1977; Morris etal, 1981), and digitalisike sub-
stances (Millett et al, 1986) ar higher inthe HTH ofthe hy-
pertensive rt than in that of the normotensive animal. Meta-
bolic research demonstrated a higher level of activity in the
HTH of SHR during the development of hypertension, com-
pared with those observed in the Wistar Kyoto strain (WKY;
Krukoff and Weigel, 1989). Histological studies have revealed

derlying mechanisms responsible fortis eflect have not been
fully cared. However, it i unlikely thatthe diminished re-
sponsiveness to noxious stimuli results from a pathological
change in primary nociceptor afferent activity, secondary to
hypertension, because the hypoalgesic response is observed fo-

thedevelopment ofhypertesion in SHR (Faden and Feuerstein,
1983; Randish and Maixner, 1984)

terize SHR were examined in the grafted rats. These changes
include myocardial hypereophy, hypoalgesia, and alterations
in VIP gene expression in the host brain. Graft development

In the induction of inflammatory reactions, two desiderata should
be satisfied: (1) The reaction produced must be readable and fairly
quantitative. (2) It should be repeatable a number of times in the
same animal. For this purpose the conjunctival mucus membrane
of the rabbit offers a sensitive surface well supplied with nerves
and blood vessels; easily observed without undue disturbance to
the animal, and anatomically constructed into a pouch capable of
holding drugs for a relatively long time. The experiments of Am-
berg, Loevenhart, and McClure," and Chiari and Januschke* show
conclusively that pure mustard oil so injures the conjunctival mucus
membrane and the cornea that only one experiment can be per-
formed on any individual animal. It is obvious that a technic
which would not produce a permanent damage to the structure of the
eye would permit a more reliable study of the effects of drugs on
the inflammatory process.

In the experiments here reported, the objections to the use of
pure mustard oil as the irritant were obviated by substitution of a
15% solution of essential oil of mustard in mineral oil. One drop
of the latter instilled into the conjunctival sac of a rabbit produced
a reaction which was readable, and from which the animal! recov-
ered completely in from 7 to 14 days, at which time the experiment
could be repeated. Some of the animals were used for as many
as 14 experiments, without permanent injury to the eye. In 114
instillations made in 27 rabbits, the following phenomena were

‘Summary. The quantitative impact of smoking on
morality and longevity in Switzeriand is investigated.
‘The calculations are based on morality ratios berween
smokers and nonsmokers reported in prospective epi-
demiologic studies, the prevalence of smoking in Swit-
zerland, cause-specific mortality rates and the lifeable,
‘The results imply that in 1976 roughly 3800 premature
deaths were attributable to smoking, more than 90%
involving males. Thus, smoking appears to be the lead-
ing preventable cause of death in Switzerland. The
results of lifetable methods used to estimate the impact
of smoking on longevity imply that smoking reduces
male life expectancy by 12 to 16 months, and that era
ication of smoking would increase life expectancy ac-
cordingly. Combining the above results suggests that,
in 1976, between $2,000 and 54.000 life years were lost
as a consequence of the smokers’ premature mortality.

CARL HASHIMOTO", SUZANNE GERTTULA and KATHRYN V. ANDERSON

Department of Molecular and Celt Biology, University of California, Berkeley, California 4720, USA

 

* Present address: Department of Cell Biology, ¥

Summary

 

Formation of the Drosophila embryo’s dorsal-ventral
pattern requires the maternal product of the Toll gene.
DNA sequence and genetic analyses together suggested
that the Toll gene product is a transmembrane protein
‘which communicates information from an extracyto-
plasmic compartment to the cytoplasm. Using antibodies,
as probes, we show that the Toll protein is a 13510? Mf,
slycoprotein which is tightly associated with embryonic
membranes. During the syneytial stage when dorsal~
‘ventral polarity is established, the maternal Tol protein
{is associated with the plasma membrane around the

 

University School of Medicine, New Haven, CE 0650, USA

entire embryo. During later embryonic stages, the Toll
protein is expressed zygotically on many cell surfaces,
possibly to promote cell adhesion. The plasma mem-
bbrane localization of the Tol! protein in the syncytial
‘embryo suggests that transmembrane signaling from the
extracellular perivitelline space to the cytoplasm is
required for establishment of the embryonic dorsal—ven-
tral pattern.

Key words: Drosophila embryo, dorsal-ventral pattern,
Tolt protein, plasma membrane.

 

Introduction

“The dorsal-ventral pattern of the Drosophila embryo’s
anatomy begins to unfold with the first morphogenetic
movement of gastrulation, when the most ventral cells
of the cellular blastoderm invaginate to form the
mesoderm and lateral and dorsal cells become the
ectoderm. From these germ layers will arise the
structures ~ muscle, nerve cord, ventral and dorsal
epidermis that characterize the dorsal-ventral pattern
(Campos-Ortega and Hartenstein, 1985). Formation of,
this pattern requires the functioning of a defined set of
matemal gene products (Anderson, 1987, 1989). An
embryo missing any of I maternal gene products
Known as the dorsal-group fails to produce ventral or
lateral structures; only dorsal structures are made,
Thecause all cells of the mutant embryo follow the
developmental path of dorsal cells of the wild-type
embryo (Anderson and Niisslein-Volhard, 1986). The
similar dorsalized phenotype resulting from the absence
Of any dorsal-group gene product suggests that these
molecules function in a common process to organize
embryonic dorsal-ventral polarity.

y the last to actin this process s the
dorsal gene product (Anderson et al. 198Sb). DNA
Sequence analyses reveal thatthe dorsal gene product is
structurally similar to the product of the avian proto-

 

 

 

 

 

50556153

 

‘oncogene c-rel and the protein NF-xB, a DNA-binding,
protein that regulates gene transcription (Steward,
1987; Ghosh et al. 1990, Kieran et af. 1990). In the
syncytial blastoderm, the dorsal protein appears in-
itially in the eytoplasin, uniformly distributed through:
out dorsal-ventral regions, and then in nuclei. Its
nuclear concentration is graded, however, with the
highest amount in ventral nuclei (Roth et al. 1989:
Rushlow et af. 1989; Steward, 1989). This gradient
presumably leads to region-specific expression of
zygotic gene products required by cells to specify the
dorsal-ventral pattern (Ferguson und Anderson, 1991).
The nuclear localization and gradient of tie dorsal
protein require the activities of all other dorsal-group
zene products. By identifying the biochemical functions
Of these molecules, it should be possible to define the
call biological pathway used to organize the dorsal
‘gradient and ultimately the embryo’s dorsal-ventral
pattern

‘Two observations suggest that one of the dorsal-
‘group gene products, Toll, has a unique role in
“organizing embryonic dorsal-ventral polarity. First, the
Toll gene is unusual because it is readily mutable to
both recessive loss-of-function and dominant gain-of-
function alleles (Anderson er al. 19856). Mothers
homozygous for a recessive allele produce dorsalized
embryos, whereas mothers carrying a dominant allele

INTRODUCTION

‘Two main approaches have been used in the past
to assess whether there is any risk associated with
exposure to ETS. One is based on epidemiology and
the other based on the quantities of smoke constituents
to which nonsmokers are exposed.

A criticism of published epidemiological studies
of ETS is that almost all failed to include a direct
‘measurement of exposure level (Coultas et al. 1989).
‘Although spousal smoking has frequently been used
as an index of exposure in these studies, the accuracy
of this approach has been questioned (Koo et al.

1987), Therefore, it is important to determine how
well reported spousal smoking correlates with direct-

icely smokers by saliva cotinine levels or detailed questioning.

been various definitions of a regular smoker. The
EPA for example defined regular smokers as those
having more than 30% of the average cotinine level

Meeting Report
Ribozymes: Use as Anti-HIV Therapeutic Molecules

BORO DROPULIC,' DAVID A. ELKINS,” JOHN 5. ROSSI,? and NAVA SARVER?*

Introduction

Since their discovery in the 1980's, the Tetrahymena group I intron (1) and RNAse P RNA (2)
catalytic RNAs (ribozymes) have provoked widespread interest from an intellectual, and more
recently, an applied direction. Many investigators, by specifically targeting deleterious

acellular RNA transcripts associated with disease, are now seeking to harness the RNA
scission and catalytic properties of diverse ribozyme motifs (hammerhead, hairpin, VS-RNA of
‘Neurospora, and hepatitis delta virus genomic and antigenomic RNA) for therapeutic application.

 

  

In the two years since the second ribozyme symposium [‘Catalytic RNA as an Anti-HIV Agent:
Design and Delivery to Cells’, October 21-24, 1990; (3)], considerable advances were made in
many experimental fronts (cell free systems, tissue culture, and in animal models) all critical for
the optimal design and delivery of ribozymes in human diseases. Notwithstanding reported
progress, several challenges remain in maximizing the therapeutic benefit of ribozymes in a
clinical setting, most critical of which are (i) enhanced cleavage efficiency; (ji) improved
rnuclease-resistance; (iii) delivery to target cell (either directly or via gene therapy using viral
vectors); (iv) delivery to specific target molecules; and (v) testing in model biological systems.
‘This article highlights significant and novel developments in ribozyme technology and the central
issues facing the application of ribozymes as intervention molecules in HIV/AIDS and other
diseases as reported at the "Third International Symposium on Catalytic RNAs (Ribozymes) and
Targeted Gene Therapy for the Treatment of HIV Infection’, December 6-11, 1992, San Diego,

focus on aspects of gene therapy addressed at the meeting.

and is0-2, of yeast eytochrome c. Infrared spectroscopy
of carbon monoxide liganded to heme ay has revealed a

ets of isoforms on TNogx is minimized with the pairs of
isoforms (iso-l-cytochromec and subunit Va or iso-2 and

those findings support the conclusion that the subunit V

Alladditional subunits are nuclear genome products. These are

‘To address the function of the nuclesr-coded subunits in

Vila, and VIID encoded by nuclear genes (COX4, COXSa or

12). COXSa and COXSb encode interchangeable isoforms, Va
and Vb, of subunit V (18, 14). The other subunits are spocifiod
by unique genes present in single copy on their respective
genomes, All six nuclear-coded subunits have primary se-
quence homology to subunits in mammalian eytochrome ¢oxi-
aces 15). Recently, a milder purification protocol has revealed
what may be three additional polypeptide subunits in yeast
cytochrome e oxidase preparations (16).
